spec_match.rationale: Three patterns aligned with the TC39 proposal's motivation: search-result merge, heterogeneous user collections, lazy prime-finding with early exit. All call `Iterator.concat()` and chain real iterator helpers.

interactive.rationale: Three pattern blocks each with a run button that exercises the pattern live and displays the output — past the static-card bar even though the surface is less rich than the pipeline builder.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises merging search results, combining log streams, and processing heterogeneous data — the three patterns deliver on this with realistic examples.

edge_cases.rationale: Pattern 3 (prime-find with early exit) is a genuine edge-case test of laziness, but the page doesn't surface the pull counts so visitors can't see early exit happening. The 'combining log streams' angle from the blurb maps onto pattern 1 only loosely — no time-ordered merge example.

references.rationale: See-also includes https://chromestatus.com/feature/5193275975794688, MDN, and the TC39 proposal.

design_system.rationale: Variables throughout, pattern-block layout uses the design system tokens, no static-card.

openQuestions.title: Add a real merge-sorted-streams pattern
openQuestions.detail: The blurb mentions 'combining log streams' but the patterns shown all concat in order, not in time order. A genuine log-merge pattern would interleave timestamps from multiple sorted sources — implement that as Pattern 4 (or as a follow-up concept) so authors see the limit of plain concat and the need for a merge-by helper.

openQuestions.title: Surface pull counts in Pattern 3
openQuestions.detail: The prime example claims early exit but the visitor only sees the output array. Add a small 'rangeA pulled X, rangeB pulled Y, rangeC pulled 0' readout so the lazy-stops-early claim is visibly verified.

openQuestions.title: Add a database-pagination pattern
openQuestions.detail: Concat-ing page after page of a cursored fetch is a real-world use case that maps onto sync `Iterator.concat()` only awkwardly. Address it explicitly: either as a deliberate non-example pointing to async iterators, or as a synchronous slice-pagination case.

summary: Three honest patterns that exercise the spec, but the lazy-stops-early claim is invisible to the visitor — surface pull counts, add a true merge-sorted pattern for log streams, and note where async iteration is the right tool.
